front: Poet: wrote "Dulce et Decorum Est," "Anthem for Doomed Youth," and "Strange Meeting" during this war, and was killed one week before its 1918 armistice
Document: the treaty that ended this war assigned blame to the loser in its "war guilt" clause, criticized by Keynes in The Economic Consequences of the Peace
Composition: this Benjamin Britten piece sets Wilfred Owen's poems and was inspired by this war
Composition: this Maurice Ravel suite has six movements, each dedicated to a friend who died in this war
Event: women handed out white feathers to men not in uniform during this war, to shame them into enlisting
Event: recruiting posters of this war included one showing a girl asking her father "Daddy, what did YOU do in the war?" and the "Lord Kitchener Wants You" poster
Event: the spiked-helmet-wearing side in this war was denounced as "huns," and its zeppelin bombing raids were called "baby-killers"
Work: recruiting music-hall songs of this war included "Keep the Home Fires Burning" (Ivor Novello), George M. Cohan's "Over There," and "It's a Long Way to Tipperary"
Poem: begins "If I should die, think this of me: That there's some corner of a foreign field / That is for ever England," published during this war
Event: this war began with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand by the Black Hand in Sarajevo
Document: a treaty guaranteeing Belgian neutrality, violated by Germany's Schlieffen Plan and derided as a "scrap of paper," brought Britain into this war in 1914
back: 1839 Treaty of London (guarantee of Belgian neutrality)

front: Document: laid out Germany's planned territorial expansion at the start of this war
Work: A.J.P. Taylor's book proposing the "Railway Thesis," arguing this war became unstoppable once mobilization began
Work: Barbara Tuchman's history of the war's first month, covering the pre-war "Willy-Nicky" letters between the Kaiser and the Tsar
Work: Christopher Clark's history of the war's origins that rejects traditional explanations of blame
Historian: German scholar who controversially argued Germany bore primary responsibility for this war, citing the Septemberprogramm (the "Fischer Controversy")
Historian: attributed this war's cause less to foreign policy and more to the aristocracy's attempt to maintain domestic power, in a thesis called the "Primacy of Domestic Politics"
Figure: led the German General Staff at this war's outset and implemented the Schlieffen Plan, before a nervous breakdown and retirement after the First Battle of the Marne
Poem: begins "In Flanders fields, the poppies blow / Between the crosses, row on row," commemorating soldiers of this war
Event: British and German soldiers legendarily played soccer during this 1914 truce
Battle: stopped the initial German advance on the Western Front early in this war, leading to Helmuth von Moltke the Younger's retirement
Battle: the Canadian Corps helped turn the tide here in 1917, part of the fighting that led to the Conscription Crisis back home
Battle: fought on the Western Front alongside Vimy Ridge; Canadian troops suffered heavy losses here
Battle: Germany's fast railway-enabled mobility led to a decisive defeat of the Russian Second Army here
Figure: Russian general who committed suicide out of shame after his army's disastrous defeat at Tannenberg
Battle: the first of two engagements near these lakes was lost by Russian general Paul von Rennenkampf
Event: Russian general Aleksei Brusilov launched this offensive, one of the deadliest operations of the war
Event: this offensive led to the "Great Retreat," leaving Galicia under enemy control and causing the Tsar to personally take command of the Russian army
Event: a six-month siege under brutal conditions during the war's Eastern Front
Document: treaty by which Russia exited this war amid the Russian Revolution, ceding most of Ukraine
Campaign: this failed campaign against the Ottoman Empire saw ANZAC forces land at ANZAC Cove and fight at Lone Pine and the Nek
work: The Gallipoli Campaign and the Middle Eastern Theater

front: Event: this April 25 holiday commemorates the Gallipoli landings with traditions like a "gunfire breakfast"
work: The Gallipoli Campaign and the Middle Eastern Theater

front: Event: T.E. Lawrence sponsored this revolt, begun by Sherif Hussein and Prince Faisal against Ottoman forces under Enver Pasha, which culminated in the capture of Damascus
work: The Gallipoli Campaign and the Middle Eastern Theater

front: Battle: General Edmund Allenby won a decisive victory here on the Nablus Plain
work: The Gallipoli Campaign and the Middle Eastern Theater

front: Figure: German general who led a small, mobile guerrilla force of Askaris in German East Africa against much larger British, French, and Portuguese armies
Battle: an invasion led by Arthur Aitken failed here despite a 5-to-1 numerical advantage, after some soldiers were swarmed by bees
Battle: a general seeking supplies crossed the Ruvuma River and decisively won this battle
Event: Charles Crewe and Charles Tombeur each led allied armies in this coordinated, successful parallel-invasion offensive
Figure: American-educated pastor who led a revolt in Nyasaland during this war
Campaign: Britain's naval blockade of Germany, enforced by the Northern and Dover Patrols, remained in force eight months after the November armistice, ending only with the Treaty of Versailles
back: British naval blockade of Germany (World War I)

front: Event: Germany introduced "kriegsbrot" (war bread) in response to this British campaign
back: British naval blockade of Germany (World War I)

front: Event: a German U-boat sank this British ocean liner in 1915, an event that inspired works by Charles Ives and Frank Bridge
Battle: this war's major naval engagement, fought between the British and German fleets
Document: decoded by Britain's Room 40, this German communique offered Mexico the return of "lost territory" in Texas in exchange for war against the US
Event: the US entered this war in 1917, breaking Woodrow Wilson's re-election slogan "He kept us out of the war"

Event: this movement, led by figures including Theodore Roosevelt and Leonard Wood, promoted the "Plattsburg Idea" of civilian training camps and gave rise to ROTC and the National Defense Act of 1916

Institution: headed by George Creel, this US body authorized "Four Minute Men" speeches and propaganda posters (including Harry Hopps's image of Germany as a crazed gorilla) to build home-front support
Case: Supreme Court case that upheld US conscription for this war, citing Emer de Vattel's Law of Nations
Event: some US soldiers conscripted for this war were sent to Arkhangelsk in this expedition to rescue the Czech Legion
Case: along with Abrams v. US, this Supreme Court case centered on the distribution of anti-war pamphlets during this war
Event: Canadian PM Robert Borden's Unionist government passed this policy and won the "khaki election," opposed by Wilfrid Laurier's Liberals and Quebec nationalist Henri Bourassa
work: Conscription and Political Divisions in the British Dominions

front: Figure: Australian PM whose proposal to conscript soldiers for this war split the Labor Party
work: Conscription and Political Divisions in the British Dominions

front: Figure: this Maori princess helped local men circumvent British conscription during World War I, believing Maori should not fight for a Crown responsible for the Treaty of Waitangi
work: Conscription and Political Divisions in the British Dominions

front: Event: this war led British women to gain the right to vote; suffragette Emmeline Pankhurst supported it while her daughter Sylvia opposed it, causing a family rift
work: Women, Suffrage, and the Home Front

front: Group: nickname for women who served on naval vessels beginning in this war; Josephine Carr became the first to die in it
work: Women, Suffrage, and the Home Front

front: Group: nickname for women whose skin turned yellow from working with TNT in munitions factories during this war
work: Women, Suffrage, and the Home Front

front: Event: this congress, chaired by Jane Addams, was convened at the Hague to protest this war
work: Women, Suffrage, and the Home Front

front: Novel: follows German soldier Paul Baumer and his classmate Kemmerich, who are encouraged to enlist by their teacher Kantorek
back: All Quiet on the Western Front (Erich Maria Remarque)

front: Novel: won the Prix Goncourt for its depiction of this war's trenches
Novel: follows an absurdly patriotic protagonist arrested for treason and sent to a madhouse before this war
Novel: Ernest Hemingway novel set in this war, in which the narrator's love interest is Catherine Barkley
Novel: Dalton Trumbo novel about a soldier of this war who loses his face and limbs and communicates via Morse code
Work: long poem by David Jones set during this war, followed by The Anathemata
Novel: trilogy fictionalizing Siegfried Sassoon's treatment by psychologist W.H.R. Rivers at Craiglockhart Hospital; its final volume, The Ghost Road, won the Booker Prize
Memoir: describes the author's service in the Voluntary Aid Detachment and her romance with Roland Leighton, who died in this war
Memoir: describes the author being shot through the chest as an assault-troop soldier during the Spring Offensive
Memoir: describes the author meeting Wilfred Owen and Siegfried Sassoon during this war
Novel: centers on shell-shocked veteran Chris Baldry, who loses his memory of his wife Kitty after serving in this war
back: The Return of the Soldier (Rebecca West)

front: Novel: a veteran of this war spends time in a park with his wife Lucrezia before committing suicide
Painting: depicts a line of blindfolded, wounded soldiers walking toward a dressing station; commissioned by the Britain War Memorials Committee
Work: etching series depicting Otto Dix's experiences fighting in this war
Painting: sardonic depiction of dead soldiers, publicized by Christopher Nevinson covering it with a banner reading "censored"
Sculpture: reflects Kathe Kollwitz's grief after her son Peter died in this war
back: Mother with Her Dead Son (Kathe Kollwitz)

front: Movement: despair over this war directly prompted the founding of this avant-garde art movement
Document: Article 231 of this treaty, usually called the "war guilt clause," formed the basis for reparations later restructured by the Dawes and Young Plans
work: Armistice, the Treaty of Versailles, and Legacy

front: Document: this 1923 treaty, which recognized the state of Turkey, was the final treaty formally ending World War I
work: Armistice, the Treaty of Versailles, and Legacy

front: Event: this 1923 occupation was justified by Germany's failure to deliver reparations goods owed under the Treaty of Versailles
work: Armistice, the Treaty of Versailles, and Legacy

front: Concept: myth that Germany lost this war due to internal betrayal by "November criminals," promulgated by Erich Ludendorff and fueling Weimar-era anti-Semitism
work: Armistice, the Treaty of Versailles, and Legacy

front: Group: homeless children left in this war's aftermath in Russia, aided by an organization founded by Anatoly Lunacharsky
work: Armistice, the Treaty of Versailles, and Legacy
